---
title: "Procedural Memory and Cognitive Loop Closure"
chapter_id: "procedural-memory-and-cognitive-loop-closure"
part_id: "planning-memory-reasoning-execution"
status: "conceptual"
draft_maturity: "v0.2 manuscript draft"
last_updated: "2026-06-24"
primary_sources:
  - "cognitive_loop_closure"
  - "rmi"
  - "rgs"
  - "benchmaxxing"
  - "talos"
  - "moecot"
  - "project_theseus_whitepaper"
  - "theseus_self_evolution_system"
  - "theseus_operator_os"
evidence_level: "argument"
claim_label: "Design rationale"
open_evidence_gaps:
  - "Chapter is a v0.2 manuscript draft; v1.0 still needs source-note substantiation for all assigned sources."
  - "No Codex tests have been implemented or run for this chapter unless separately recorded in Appendix E."
  - "No support-state promotion is implied by this drafting pass."
---

# Procedural Memory and Cognitive Loop Closure

## Chapter status

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Chapter ID | `procedural-memory-and-cognitive-loop-closure` |
| Part | Part II - Planning, Memory, Reasoning, and Execution |
| Status | conceptual |
| Manuscript maturity | v0.2 manuscript draft |
| Last updated | 2026-06-24 |
| Primary source records | `cognitive_loop_closure`, `rmi`, `rgs`, `benchmaxxing`, `talos`, `moecot`, `project_theseus_whitepaper`, `theseus_self_evolution_system`, `theseus_operator_os` |
| Claim label | Design rationale |
| Evidence level | argument |
| Source queue | primary: `cognitive_loop_closure`; supporting: `rmi`, `rgs`, `benchmaxxing`, `talos`, `project_theseus_whitepaper`, `theseus_self_evolution_system`, `theseus_operator_os`; connector/recovery: `moecot` |
| Source loading state | source notes: `cognitive_loop_closure`, `rmi`, `rgs`, `benchmaxxing`, `talos`, `moecot`, `project_theseus_whitepaper`, `theseus_self_evolution_system`, `theseus_operator_os`; raw cache: `cognitive_loop_closure`, `rmi`, `rgs`, `benchmaxxing`, `talos`; connector/recovery: `moecot` |
| Test state | Planned only; no tests have been run for this chapter in this drafting pass. |

## Drafting guardrail

This chapter is a v0.2 living-book draft. It synthesizes the architecture manifest, the current outline, the source inventory, available source notes, and local raw-source readiness. It does not by itself promote any claim beyond the support state shown in the chapter metadata and Appendix C. Where the chapter uses design reasoning, it is written as architecture argument rather than as reported empirical fact.

## Problem

Repeated reasoning trajectories should not be improvised forever when they can become verified procedures or tools.

Within the ASI Stack, this is an operational cognition layer problem. The layer receives governed goals, command contracts, memory packets, beliefs, work orders, and tool requests and must turn them into typed plans, context selections, claims, jobs, artifacts, approvals, and replayable execution records. The hard part is not naming the capability; it is making the boundary explicit enough that a later layer can test, replay, reject, or replace it without guessing what authority was assumed.

It inherits pressure from the previous chapter, Runtime Adapters, Tool Permissions, and Human Approval, and makes that pressure operational in a narrower boundary. It closes this part by making the remaining handoff to the next part explicit. That is why the chapter is written as part of a single stack rather than as a standalone paper summary.

## Why existing approaches are insufficient

Agents waste cognition and introduce inconsistency when recurring workflows are repeatedly reconstructed from scratch.

In this chapter, the failure is more specific than bad prompting or weak model choice: agents waste cognition and introduce inconsistency when recurring workflows are repeatedly reconstructed from scratch. If `procedural-memory-and-cognitive-loop-closure` is left implicit, downstream layers inherit a capability whose authority, evidence burden, and rollback conditions are unclear. The stack therefore treats the missing contract as the defect, not merely the local model behavior.

This does not mean that every layer must be heavyweight. The stack is efficient precisely because small, typed artifacts can carry constraints forward while large models, tools, humans, or specialist modules are invoked only where the current boundary demands them.

## Core Claim

[procedural-memory-and-cognitive-loop-closure.core, label: Design rationale, support: argument] Cognitive loop closure compiles repeated reasoning into verified parameterized tools and procedural memory.

The claim is architectural: it says how this layer should be shaped if the whole system is expected to remain governed under growth. The current support state is `argument`, so the claim should be read as a design rationale unless a later evidence bundle promotes it. The assigned source set - Cognitive Loop Closure, Ratcheting Modular Intelligence, Ratcheting Generative Systems, Benchmaxxing: The Performance Ratchet, and Talos Protocol - gives the chapter its vocabulary and comparison points, but the public manuscript keeps source-derived support separate from the draft itself.

## Mechanism

The mechanism turns the core claim into a working contract. First, log safe execution traces. Second, detect recurring loops. Third, abstract invariant structure, discover parameters, verify, and publish tools.

- Log safe execution traces.
- Detect recurring loops.
- Abstract invariant structure, discover parameters, verify, and publish tools.

Operationally, the layer should produce artifacts that can be inspected without rerunning the whole cognition path. A downstream reader or executor should be able to locate the relevant inputs, constraints, residuals, authority grants, and failure conditions. That artifact discipline is what lets the stack improve without making the improvement path opaque.

For implementation, the mechanism should be kept small at first: a schema, a ledger entry, a route decision, a context packet, a proof obligation, or a replayable job record. Only after that minimal form is testable should the layer absorb richer heuristics, learned routing, larger memory, or autonomous repair.

## Interfaces

The interface for Procedural Memory and Cognitive Loop Closure is the contract that lets adjacent layers compose without absorbing each other's authority. It should make the chapter's output legible to planning, memory, reasoning, execution, evidence, and improvement layers while preserving the boundary described by the core claim.

- Artifact graph supplies traces.
- Routing selects new tools.
- Evidence tests utility and regressions.

The upstream interface accepts the part-level inputs: governed goals, command contracts, memory packets, beliefs, work orders, and tool requests. The downstream interface emits typed plans, context selections, claims, jobs, artifacts, approvals, and replayable execution records. When either side is ambiguous, the layer should fail closed by producing a missing-contract, missing-evidence, or missing-authority record instead of silently continuing.

This interface also makes chapter insertion and reordering safer. A new chapter can be added when it introduces a genuinely new contract, invariant, or artifact type; otherwise it should merge into the layer that already owns the boundary.

## Invariants

The invariants are the conditions that must remain true even when the implementation changes, the source set grows, or later chapters introduce stronger mechanisms.

- Only verified loops become tools.
- Parameters and assumptions are explicit.
- Tool promotion records residuals and regressions.

If an invariant cannot be checked directly, the living-book version of the stack should at least record a proxy check and a residual. A weak proxy is acceptable in v0.2 when it is visible; it becomes dangerous only when the manuscript treats it as a completed proof.

## Failure modes

The failure model is part of the design, not an afterthought. This layer is expected to fail in ways that can be detected, quarantined, and used to improve the architecture.

- Premature toolification.
- Hidden assumptions.
- Procedural memory poisoning.

For Procedural Memory and Cognitive Loop Closure, mitigation starts by turning each listed failure into a named reviewable artifact. When `Premature toolification` appears, the system should emit a residual, rejection, quarantine, regression, or human-review record rather than allowing the failure to disappear into later fluent output.

## Minimal implementation

A loop closure pipeline from trace detection to verified tool record.

The smallest useful implementation is deliberately narrow: A loop closure pipeline from trace detection to verified tool record. It should be easy to validate, easy to render in the book, and easy for a later coding run to convert into a schema, Lean predicate, deterministic fixture, trace table, or replayable command.

The implementation should be promoted only when its evidence exists. A planned test remains planned; a proof target remains planned; a prototype claim remains unpromoted until the code, command, environment, and result are recorded.

## Codex test plan

| Test | Purpose | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Loop detection test | Probe whether the chapter claim holds under the relevant Procedural Memory and Cognitive Loop Closure boundary. | planned; not run |
| Tool abstraction test | Probe whether the chapter claim holds under the relevant Procedural Memory and Cognitive Loop Closure boundary. | planned; not run |
| Verified tool regression test | Probe whether the chapter claim holds under the relevant Procedural Memory and Cognitive Loop Closure boundary. | planned; not run |

These tests are acceptance targets for later implementation work. They are not reported results. When a test is implemented, the chapter should link to the command, fixture, environment notes, and result summary, and Appendix E should be regenerated or updated accordingly.

### Formalization hooks

| Tag | Module | Target | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| `lean:procedural.loop_closure.operational_invariant` | `AsiStackProofs.ProceduralMemory` | A generated tool records its source traces, parameters, and verification result. | planned |
| `lean:procedural.loop_closure.failure_blocks_promotion` | `AsiStackProofs.ProceduralMemory` | A tool with failed regression cannot be promoted to routable status. | planned |

The table records proof and contract hooks for future work. Planned hooks should become Lean proofs only when the predicate is precise enough to mechanize; otherwise they should become schemas, process contracts, or research-agenda items.

## Source crosswalk

| Source ID | Title | Layer | Planned use | Readiness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `cognitive_loop_closure` | Cognitive Loop Closure | procedural_memory | Repeated cognition should become procedural memory / verified tools. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `rmi` | Ratcheting Modular Intelligence | capability_ratchet | Benchmark pressure, residual escrow, verified modular capability, regression preservation. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `rgs` | Ratcheting Generative Systems | compression_capability_growth | Bridge between active compression, procedural memory, benchmark frontiers, verified AI growth. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `benchmaxxing` | Benchmaxxing: The Performance Ratchet | benchmarks_evidence | Benchmarks as pressure surfaces, saturation -> regression, harder frontier, anti-Goodhart safeguards. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `talos` | Talos Protocol | labor_execution_os | AI labor OS. Deterministic cognitive manufacturing, typed jobs, control planes, auditability, tool isolation. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `moecot` | MoECOT-Agent Architecture Whitepaper | implementation_reference | Concrete implementation evidence: governed low-parameter multi-core runtime, readiness gates, ledgers, replay. | source note available; connector or recovery required |
| `project_theseus_whitepaper` | Project Theseus Whitepaper | report_first_rmi_prototype | Local-first report-driven RMI implementation reference: SymLiquid, SparkStream, Octopus Router, residual escrow, self-evolution gates, Hive runtime, observability. | source note available |
| `theseus_self_evolution_system` | Theseus Self-Evolution System | recursive_self_improvement_governance | Evidence-first self-evolution lane with intervention ladder, ATTD repo-health gate, guarded teacher self-edit, architecture experiment governance, loop closure, and outcome ledger. | source note available |
| `theseus_operator_os` | Hive Operator OS and Work Board | labor_os_operator_surface | Shared command vocabulary, durable SQLite work board, node registry, background/watch/wake contracts, skill registry, tool hooks, feedback routing, and safety-visible operator surface. | source note available |

The crosswalk describes why each source is assigned to the chapter and what is currently available in the repo. A local raw cache or source note makes future mining easier, but this draft still keeps the support state conservative until the specific claim-to-source mapping is recorded.

## Summary

Procedural Memory and Cognitive Loop Closure defines one boundary in the ASI Stack: Cognitive loop closure compiles repeated reasoning into verified parameterized tools and procedural memory. Its job is to make a capability more governable, not merely more impressive.

In v0.2, this chapter gives Procedural Memory and Cognitive Loop Closure a complete architectural boundary: repeated reasoning trajectories should not be improvised forever when they can become verified procedures or tools. Its next maturity step is to substantiate the boundary through `Artifact graph supplies traces`, preserve `Only verified loops become tools`, and replace planned proof/test hooks with validated artifacts. Claims should move only when source notes, code, proofs, or run records justify the movement.
